concepts.title: Heat Capacity
concepts.conceptBody:
Heat Capacity (Thermal capacity) C'

The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of a substance of $1\text{C}$ (or $1\text{ K}$)

$\text{C'}=\frac{\Delta\text{ Q}}{\Delta\text{ T}}\Rightarrow \Delta\text{ Q}=\text{C' }\Delta\text{ T}~(\text{Avg}\text{. }\text{ value) }$

But, strictly $\text{C'}$ is not constant for a temperature change.

Unit : J/ C or $\text{J}/\text{K}$

Absolute Value : $\left( C'=\frac{dQ}{dT} \right)$

$\therefore \text{ dQ}=\text{C'dT}$

$\therefore \Delta\text{ Q}=\int _{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}\text{C'dT}$ Take the limits in given range with proper scale $(0\text{C}$ or $\text{K})$

concepts.title: Molar Heat Capacity
concepts.conceptBody:
Molar Heat Capacity (C)

The amount of heat required to increase the temp. of $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ mol}$ of a substance of $1\text{C}$ or $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$.

$\text{C}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}}{\text{n }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ T}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}=\text{nC }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ T}\left( \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Avg}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ value }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \right)$

Absolute value $\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}=\int _{{{\text{T}}_{1}}}^{{{\text{T}}_{2}}}\text{nCdT }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ units : $\text{J}/\text{mol}/\text{K}$

concepts.title: Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.conceptBody:
Specific Heat Capacity (S)

The amount of heat required to raise the temperature of unit mass of a substance by $1\text{C}$ (or $1\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ K}$ ).

\(\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{S}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{m} \Delta \mathrm{T}} \Rightarrow \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\mathrm{ms} \Delta \mathrm{T}~(\text {Avg.value}) \\ \Delta \mathrm{Q}=\int_{\mathrm{T}_1}^{\mathrm{T}_2} \operatorname{msdT}~(\text {Absolute value) unit: } \mathrm{J} / \mathrm{kg} / \mathrm{K}\end{array}\)

concepts.title: Latent Heat of Fusion and Vapourization
concepts.conceptBody:
Latent Heat of Fusion and Vapourization

Latent Heat : The amount of heat required to change the state of unit mass of a substance at constant temperature is called latent heat.

$\begin{matrix} \text{L}=\frac{\text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}}{\text{m}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ unit }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }:\left( \text{J}/\text{Kg} \right) \\ \therefore \text{ }\!\!\Delta\!\!\text{ Q}=\text{mL} \\ \end{matrix}$

(1) Latent Heat of Fusion : The amount of heat required to change the state of unit mass of substance from solid to liquid form at its melting point is called latent heat of fusion.

(2) Latent Heat of Vaporization : The amount of heat required to change the state of a unit mass of substance from liquid to vapour form at its boiling point is called latent heat of vaporization.

Principle of Calorimetry : 

Total heat lost by system $=$ Total heat gained by system [for an isolated system]

concepts.title: Water Equivalent
concepts.conceptBody:
Water Equivalent (W)

It is the mass of water to which if same amount of heat is given, cause the same temp. change as that of the body.

$\mathrm{C}^{\prime}=\mathrm{m} \quad \mathrm{S}$

= (mass) (specific heat of water)

(Replace the body by same amount of water)

concepts.title: Relation Between Heat Capacity, Molar Heat Capacity and Specific Heat Capacity
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation between $\mathbf{C},\text{C'}$ and S

$\text{C'}=\text{mS}=\text{nC}$

(i) Thermal capacity $=($mass) (specific heat capacity)

$=\left( \text{ moles } \right)\left( \text{ molar }\text{ heat }\text{ capacity} \right)$

(ii) $\text{C}=\text{MS}$

Molar heat capacity $=($molecular mass) (specific heat capacity)

concepts.title: Law of Mixtures
concepts.conceptBody:
Relation between ${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{P}}},{{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{V}}}$ and γ

$\gamma_{\mathrm{g}}=3 \alpha_{\mathrm{g}}=27 \quad 10^{-6} / \mathrm{C}$

$\mathrm{U}=\mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KT}\right) \rightarrow$ for 1 molecule

for $\mathrm{n}$ moles of a gas $\mathrm{U}=\left(\mathrm{n} \mathrm{N}_{\mathrm{A}}\right) \mathrm{f}\left(\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{KT}\right)=\mathrm{n}\left(\frac{\mathrm{fR}}{2} \mathrm{~T}\right)$

$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\frac{\mathrm{dU}}{\mathrm{ndT}}=\frac{\mathrm{nfR} / 2}{\mathrm{ndT}} \mathrm{dT}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{fR}}{2}\right)$

$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{fR}}{2}\right)$

$\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}=\left(\frac{\mathrm{fR}}{2}+\mathrm{R}\right)=\left(\frac{\mathrm{f}}{2}+1\right) \mathrm{R}$

$\gamma=\frac{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{P}}}{\mathrm{C}_{\mathrm{V}}}=\frac{(\mathrm{f}+2) \mathrm{R}(2)}{2 \mathrm{fR}}=\left(1+\frac{2}{\mathrm{f}}\right)$

for monoatomic gas 

${{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}=\frac{3\text{R}}{2},{{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}=\frac{5\text{R}}{2},\gamma =\left( \frac{5}{3} \right)$

for diatomic gas (also $\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$ ) : 

${{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}=\frac{5\text{R}}{2},{{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}}=\frac{7\text{R}}{2},\left( \gamma =7/5 \right)$

${{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{P}}},{{\mathbf{C}}_{\mathbf{V}}}$ and γ for a mixture of gases 

Let ${{n}_{1}}$ mol of monoatomic, ${{n}_{2}}$ mol of diatomic, ${{n}_{3}}$ mol of gas mixture be present whose temperature is increased by $\text{dT}$ at constant volume.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}} {} & \left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{1}}}}\text{dT}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{2}}}}\text{dT}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{3}}}}\text{dT} \right)=\left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}} \right){{\text{C}}_{\text{Vmix}}} \\ {} & ~\therefore {{\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}} \right)}_{\text{mix }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\left( \frac{{{\text{n}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{1}}}}+\text{n}2{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{2}}}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{V}}_{3}}}}}{{{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}} \right) \\ \end{array}$

Similarly, if you increase temperature by dT at constant pressure you get

${{\left( {{\text{C}}_{\text{P}}} \right)}_{\text{mix }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}}=\left( \frac{{{\text{n}}_{1}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{P}}_{1}}}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{P}}_{2}}}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}{{\text{C}}_{{{\text{P}}_{3}}}}}{{{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}} \right)$

for $\left( {{\gamma }_{\text{mixture }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)$,

$\left( {{\gamma }_{\text{mixture }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}} \right)=\frac{{{C}_{\text{P}}}\left( \text{mix} \right)}{{{\text{C}}_{\text{V}}}\left( \text{mix} \right)}$

or, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\text{n}}_{1}}\text{R}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{1}^{-1}}}} \right)}\text{dT}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{2}}\left( \text{R} \right)}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{2}^{-1}}}} \right)}\text{dT}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{3}}\text{RdT}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{3}^{-1}}}} \right)}=\left( {{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}} \right)\frac{\text{R}d\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ T}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{\text{mi}{{\text{x}}^{-1}}}} \right)}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{{{\text{n}}_{1}}+{{\text{n}}_{2}}+{{\text{n}}_{3}}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{\text{mix}{{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }}^{-1}}}} \right)}=\frac{{{\text{n}}_{1}}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{1}^{-1}}}} \right)}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{2}}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{2}^{-1}}}} \right)}+\frac{{{\text{n}}_{3}}}{\left( {{\gamma }_{{{3}^{-1}}}} \right)}$

If $220 \mathrm{~g}$ of hot water at $70^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is poured in the vessel, the final temperature neglecting radiation loss, will be

The temperature when $A$ and $B$ are mixed is $16^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and when $B$ and $C$ are mixed is $23^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature when $A$ and $C$ are mixed is
